data.title: Stock Pickers: Lights, Camera, Anticlimax?
data.description:
U.S. equities have been whipsawed by tariff fears in the past couple of months, and particularly over the past week, with sharp double-digit swings for the S&P 500. The index’s slide into correction territory has coincided with a rise in implied volatility, with VIX rising briefly above the 50-point handle. In environments characterized by both market declines and high volatility, we typically hear that active management can have an advantage over index funds.

While the current macro environment is unusual, to say the least, we can look to history for a better understanding of active manager performance trends. We start by analyzing the 24-year history of our SPIVA® U.S. Scorecard, where we measure the performance of active managers versus their appropriate benchmarks. Exhibit 1 shows that 5 out of these 24 years were characterized by market declines. Notably, all five years coincided with majority underperformance for large-cap funds, ranging from 51% in 2022 to 68% in 2002.

But how has the performance of active managers during market declines compared relative to their performance during market gains? A simple way to analyze the conditions for stock selection is to measure the percentage of constituents that beat the benchmark. Exhibit 2 illustrates that, on average, 56% of member stocks beat The 500™ during the five years when the index declined, outpacing the 46% during the 19 years when the market posted gains. This makes sense, as down markets can provide an easier hurdle for stocks to beat.

Despite this advantage, 61% of large-cap funds underperformed The 500 during down markets, only slightly better than the 65% underperformance during up markets and the 64% average across all 24 years.

As well as market downturns, the other potentially advantageous element for active managers to examine is the rise in volatility and specifically dispersion, which measures how differently stocks are performing relative to each other. The value of stock-selection skill rises when dispersion is high, which could mean greater opportunities for stock pickers to outperform.

In Exhibit 3, we divided the years in our SPIVA database into low and high dispersion periods, defined as when the benchmark S&P 500’s dispersion was below the historical 25th percentile and above the 75th percentile, respectively. As expected, large-cap managers generally fared better in high dispersion periods, with 56% underperforming the S&P 500, lower than the 67% during low dispersion periods. Still, high dispersion regimes were characterized by majority underperformance.

In addition to analyzing historical dispersion environments, we can also look to implied dispersion to understand the potential for future opportunities for stock selection. We observe in Exhibit 4 that the Cboe S&P 500 Dispersion Index (DSPX), which uses listed options to measures the expectations for dispersion over the next 30 calendar days, reached an all-time live high of 46.5 on April 10, 2025, and is currently just below the 40-point handle. This level means that the market expects that the spread of annualized S&P 500 stock returns will have a standard deviation of close to 40% next month, which could signify plentiful future potential prospects for active stock selection.

History tells us that although active managers tended to fare better during market declines and high dispersion periods, majority underperformance prevailed regardless of market conditions. The future dispersion environment may be fortuitous, but the question of the hour is whether stock pickers will be able to shine during these unique circumstances. Thanks to our SPIVA Scorecards, we will be watching.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Meta: A Likely Downward Adjustment Of Capex Could Start A Bullish Rally
data.description:
Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) stock has seen a big correction after the stock hit its peak in mid-February. This correction is similar to what we saw in other Mag 7 stocks in the last few weeks as recession fears grow and tariffs are reducing the bullish sentiment on Wall Street. However, despite this correction, Meta continues to show fundamental strength. Besides recession fears, one of the key reasons behind this correction could be the massive capex projection announced by Meta to build its AI infrastructure. Meta estimated $60 billion to $65 billion in capex for 2025, up from $39 billion in 2024. We have seen cheaper AI models launched almost every week since the DeepSeek shock. Wall Street is rightly concerned about the ROI of these massive investments within a reasonable time period. In the previous article, it was mentioned that Meta's AI monetization could accelerate in the next few quarters.

Meta has used its own AI models to launch new AI tools for advertisers. This has given the advertisers a better conversion rate for their ads. It has also helped Meta improve the price per ad over the last few quarters. In Q4 2024, Meta reported 14% YoY growth in price per ad, which helped boost the revenue and earnings of the company.

After the recent correction, Meta stock is trading at less than 16 times the EPS estimate for fiscal year ending Dec 2027. The forward EPS growth projections continue to be very good. While the recession fears are quite strong, Meta has a good moat and should be able to weather any near-term headwinds. The modest P/E multiple increases the attractiveness of the stock compared to other peers.

A Roller Coaster Ride

Meta stock saw a strong bullish momentum in the first few weeks of 2025, reaching a peak in mid-February. Since then, the stock has dropped significantly. Meta’s earnings report was very positive, with the company easily beating the EPS and revenue estimates. I believe that one of the key reasons behind the bearish sentiment towards Meta could be the massive capex hike projected by the management.

Meta has announced $60 billion to $65 billion of capex in 2025. This announcement was made close to the DeepSeek announcement in late January. The massive cost optimization reported by DeepSeek has changed the optics for the AI investments. We continue to hear about new AI models almost every week, which are cheaper and better than earlier models. This raises the question regarding the need for massive AI investments and the ability of Meta to deliver a good ROI from these investments.

In 2024, Meta had a capex of $39.2 billion, and in 2023 it was $28 billion. It can be difficult for the management to efficiently deploy the massive capex. Meta is already investing heavily in Reality Labs, in which it has reported over $50 billion in losses since 2020. Hence, a big capex hike could be a headwind for the company in the near term. The Q4 2024 earnings report was close to the announcement of DeepSeek, which limited the ability of the management to make big changes in their projection of investments. It is likely that we could see more clarity about capex and AI investment in the upcoming earnings.

The current tariff situation could be a good excuse for the management to moderate its massive capex plans for 2025. A reduction in capex could also improve the sentiment towards the stock as the company focuses more on making sure the ROI is good before buying the latest AI chips.

Customized AI Tools Deliver A Better Price Per Ad

Meta is in a good position to build new AI tools for advertisers and monetize them quickly. These AI tools help in better targeting of ads. Meta is also looking to build an Agentic AI tool for small businesses, which will help them reach their customers more efficiently at a lower cost. Some of these initiatives have already shown good results. We have seen a steady growth in Meta’s price per ad over the last few quarters.

It is difficult to increase ad impressions beyond a point, as it can hurt the user experience. The worldwide YoY growth in ad impressions over the last few quarters has seen a decline. The YoY growth in ad impressions in Q4 2023 was 21%. This steadily declined to 6% YoY growth in Q4 2024. We might continue to see a modest growth in this metric over the next few quarters. However, the faster price per ad growth has made up for the slowdown in ad impressions growth.

Modest P/E Multiple And Good Growth Momentum

The consensus EPS estimate for fiscal year ending Dec 2027 is $33.24, which gives the stock a forward P/E multiple of only 15.99. This is quite modest when we look at the top line and EPS growth projections for the next few years. It should be noted that the heavy losses in Reality Labs are already priced in. The trailing twelve-month losses in Reality Labs were close to $17 billion. While the operating income of the Family of Apps segment has increased over the last few quarters, the losses from the Reality Labs segment have stabilized. This has helped Meta report better operating income.

Meta faces a number of headwinds in the near term. The recession fears will continue to be a big macro challenge, which can hurt the overall spending on the platform. However, the forward growth momentum is still quite good while the P/E multiple is very modest.

We can see in the above image that Meta is estimated to deliver double-digit revenue growth for the next few years. This is quite good considering the negative macro sentiment due to the tariff situation. It will be important to see the management's comment on capex and any possible changes. I believe it is highly likely that Meta will make some downward adjustment to earlier capex guidance due to the changing economic situation and the launch of cheaper AI tools.

Investor Takeaway

Meta stock has seen a correction in the last few weeks. Some of this is due to broader recession and tariff headwinds. However, Wall Street is also concerned about the ability of Meta to deploy its massive capex efficiently. We could get more clarity on the future capex roadmap of Meta in the upcoming earnings. Cheaper and better AI tools have reduced the need to make massive investments in AI infrastructure.

Meta has been able to deliver good price per ad growth over the last few quarters. It is likely that this growth momentum will continue as new AI tools are launched for advertisers, which will help in better-targeted ads and better conversions from ads. Meta stock is trading at less than 16 times the EPS for fiscal year ending Dec 2027, which is quite modest when we compare with other Mag 7 stocks, making the stock a Buy at the current price.

data.title: U.S. Bancorp: Reassuring Q1 Results Make Me Look Beyond Tariff Woes
data.description:
Introduction

In January, when I last covered major super-regional bank U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB), the stock was still trading in the high-$40 range. I discussed the current state of the bank's business and outlined my modestly bullish strategy for USB stock, involving out-of-the-money puts.

Against the backdrop of an increasingly likely recession and the associated expectation of rising defaults and lower interest rates, bank stocks were hammered in response to the tariffs announced by President Trump two weeks ago. Naturally, amid the sharp rise in uncertainty, option premiums spiked, making the sale of puts much more profitable than previously expected.

Of course, the secondary effects associated with the tariffs would certainly impact the bank's earnings, but I would argue that this is (more than) reflected in the market's reaction. As shown in Figure 1, USB shares are currently trading at a 20% discount to their average valuation since 2020 - a period that I don't think is exactly representative of a normal economic cycle. The stock therefore looks even more attractive compared to its long-term average valuation. This is also reflected in the earnings-based valuation shown in Figure 2.

However, before running out and selling put options on USB shares or buying the stock outright, I think a fresh look at the company's fundamentals is warranted, especially in light of today's Q1 2025 earnings report.

Let's take a look at how the bank has performed so far in 2025…

U.S. Bancorp – Reassuring Q1 2025 Results Make Me Look Beyond Tariff Woes

For the first quarter of 2025, U.S. Bancorp exceeded both earnings per share and net revenue estimates - by 5% and 0.7%, respectively. Unlike last year, when the bank reported significant merger and integration-related charges (acquisition of MUFG Union Bank) and an increase in the FDIC special assessment, EPS of $1.03 reported for the first quarter of 2025 does not include any adjustments. Factoring in the above adjustments for Q1 2024, EPS increased 12.6% year-over-year. As a result, U.S. Bancorp again reported a really solid return on common equity (ROTCE) of 17.5%, up 2.4 percentage points year-over-year, but virtually unchanged compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.

It was reassuring to see that U.S. Bancorp reported significant growth in net revenues in the first quarter of 2025, well ahead of expense growth.

Revenue growth in the first quarter was driven by both net interest income and non-interest income growth. The former grew primarily due to loan growth (+2.1% year-over-year and +0.9% quarter-over-quarter, Figure 3), but also due to a slight increase in the net interest margin to 2.72%, due in part to fixed asset repricing.

At least as important is the 5.0% year-over-year growth in non-interest income. After all, non-interest income accounted for more than 40% of USB's net revenues in the first quarter of 2025, and diversified income streams are a key pillar of my investment thesis in this bank. Compared to the fourth quarter of 2024, non-interest income was essentially flat, which suggests to me that the solid year-over-year growth is largely due to price increases in the bank's payments, trust, and wealth management services.

On the expense side, the overall decline of 5.1% year-over-year and 1.8% quarter-over-quarter obviously looks great, but should be seen in the context of one-offs in Q1 2024. On a normalized basis, non-interest expenses actually increased slightly. Nevertheless, the slower increase in expenses compared to net revenue growth points to positive operating leverage. In fact, the bank achieved an operating leverage of 270 basis points, the third consecutive quarter of expansion. Importantly, however, the positive operating leverage was not achieved by a short-sighted reduction in marketing, business development, and technology expenses.

For a bank that used to be known for its strong and resilient profitability but has delivered rather disappointing results in recent years, I am pleased to see U.S. Bancorp moving in the right direction. This can also be seen in the efficiency ratio, which improved by 70 basis points quarter-over-quarter (Figure 4) and 170 basis points year-over-year (again, considering the adjustments for Q1 2024). On a side note, the numbers illustrated in Figure 4 do not include adjustments.

Another reason for the solid year-over-year earnings growth is U.S. Bancorp's continued healthy asset base. Provisions for loan losses decreased 2.9% year-over-year and 4.1% quarter-over-quarter. The further decline in the ratio of nonperforming assets was encouraging to see as well (Figure 5). U.S. Bancorp's charge-off ratio also appears to have stabilized (at least for now) at a very healthy level of below 0.6%, which is below the national average (Figure 6). Delinquent loan ratios remained stable or even improved, depending on the domain (see tables on p. 11 of the Q1 2025 earnings press release).

U.S. Bancorp remains well capitalized and had a Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio of 10.8% at the end of the quarter, up 20 basis points from the prior quarter and 80 basis points from a year ago.

Management has already indicated that it expects to return to meaningful share buybacks in the near future. Recall that back in September, a $5 billion share buyback program was authorized by USB's board of directors. However, given the recent significant increase in macroeconomic uncertainty, I expect a focus on capital preservation in an effort to maintain a healthy loss-absorbing capacity.

Importantly, I do not believe the dividend is at risk of suspension - primarily due to strong capitalization, the healthy loan portfolio, and ultimately a payout ratio currently around 50%. In fact, I would argue that a further $0.01 increase in the quarterly dividend is a reasonable expectation, assuming of course that the macroeconomic outlook does not materially deteriorate. I expect management to announce the intention to increase the dividend increase after the upcoming stress test, the scenarios for which were announced back in February.

Finally, I think it's worth noting that deposits trended somewhat weakly in Q1 2025, as shown in Figure 7 - but this is by no means a cause for concern. The most notable decline was - quite obviously - in non-interest-bearing deposits (-4.0% quarter-over-quarter), followed by time deposits (-1.9% quarter-over-quarter) and savings deposits (-0.4% quarter-over-quarter), reflecting lower balances (but not account closures) in most areas. Given the higher volatility in equities, the likely continued high market uncertainty, and ultimately the rebound in longer-term interest rates, I expect to see stabilization in the second quarter, at least in U.S. Bancorp's wealth and retail accounts.

Conclusion

In response to President Trump's recent tariff announcements, the entire banking sector saw sharp declines, and despite its fundamentally conservative positioning and focus on domestic activities, major super-regional bank U.S. Bancorp was not spared.

Overall, USB shares fell 35% from their 52-week high reached shortly after Trump's election victory and, despite a modest recovery, are still trading almost 30% below their peak. With a blended price-to-earnings ratio of below 10, a price-to-book ratio of 1, and a price-to-tangible book ratio of 1.4, the market is pricing in significant economic headwinds. Even compared to the already quite conservative five-year average valuation (which includes the pandemic and the 2023 banking crisis), USB shares are currently trading at a discount of 20%.

Given the current uncertainty and the deteriorating macroeconomic outlook, such a valuation discount seems naturally justified. After all, a recession would bring with it a significant increase in loan defaults, higher unemployment, increasingly restrained demand for new loans, and, of course, a compression in the net interest margin as a result of interest rate cuts. The high uncertainty among company CEOs - as understandable as it is in view of the latest events - does not help either.

However, I believe that USB shares look increasingly attractive with a long-term investment horizon. The first quarter results published today have strengthened my confidence in the bank's financial stability. This is underlined by the further improved CET1 ratio, charge-offs which remain below the national average, and a very manageable nonperforming assets ratio. Loan growth was solid, but the most important takeaway was continued disciplined cost management. While expenses increased, they again lagged net revenue growth, resulting in operating leverage of +270 basis points, which was notably not achieved through a short-sighted reduction in marketing, business development, and technology expenses.

With such a solid earnings report, it seems reasonable to conclude that U.S. Bancorp will finally return to meaningful share buybacks. Recall that USB's board approved a $5 billion share buyback program in September. However, given the heightened macroeconomic uncertainty and knowing the bank's cautious management culture, I expect share repurchases to either be kept to a minimum (to offset stock-based compensation) or suspended altogether. I would argue that the bank could afford to buy back shares, and at the current valuation the return would be pretty solid, but given the macroeconomic uncertainty, I really do not mind a continued focus on a strong capital base.

Depending on how the volatility of USB shares develops going forward, I can imagine continuing to sell puts. However, as my current options exposure is likely to result in a delivery of shares that will bring my position size to well over 1% of my portfolio, I am playing it safe and therefore only plan to sell relatively far out-of-the-money puts.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Airline Stocks Have Plunged In 2025: Value Trap Or Deep Value?
data.description:
Airline stocks have plunged so far this year amidst recession and political fears. American Airlines (AAL) is down 42% year-to-date, Delta Air Lines (DAL) is down 31%, and United Airlines (UAL) is down 30%. Many experienced traders regard airlines as a sort of canary in the coal mine due to their unique sensitivity to the economy and external events like terrorism, war, and pandemics. However, other traders are intrigued by the fact that these stocks are trading at 5x-7x earnings, potentially offering a high-upside deep value play on a soft landing. I'd generally favor the former explanation, given the high debt loads that airlines are carrying and continued signs of economic weakening.

Are Airlines A Value Trap or Deep Value Play?

Analyst airline stock math:

UAL 2025 earnings estimates: $10.55, P/E of roughly 6.4x

DAL 2025 earnings estimates: $5.34, P/E of roughly 7.7x

AAL 2025 earnings estimates: $1.50, P/E of roughly 6.6x

Why would the market offer this kind of opportunity? These P/E ratios imply returns of 15-20% annually. And there are tons of retail investors who buy stocks off of P/E stock screens, and heck, there are a ton of mutual fund managers who do the same. But the stocks keep getting cheaper.

There's an old school of thought with cyclical stocks like airlines, banks, and oil companies that was popularized by investing legend Peter Lynch. The idea is that when their P/E ratios are low, and they appear cheap, that's actually when they're expensive. And when their P/E ratios are high, that's because their earnings are cyclically low, and that's when you should buy. Needless to say, most investors do the opposite.

I vote that airline stocks are a value trap. However, if the aggregate analyst estimates are somehow right, I'll note that there's a ton of upside.

Warren Buffett famously quipped in his 2007 annual letter to shareholders that a farsighted capitalist would have done better to shoot the Wright Brothers down, as that's how much money has been lost in airline stocks over the years. But we'll look at both sides–not only to evaluate whether airline stocks are an opportunity, but also for what they might be saying about the US economy going forward.

Airline Stocks Are A Cruel Mistress

From the August lows, airline stocks surged last year on hopes of a strong 2025 economy and continued boom in international travel post-COVID. American Airlines stock more than doubled off its lows, and United Airlines stock nearly tripled. Analysts raised profit estimates again and again for 2025. Now it looks like investor optimism went too far. Conditions seemed fairly favorable with lower oil prices and strong demand, but things have recently turned for the worse.

Delta Air Lines withdrew profit guidance last week, and its CEO told CNBC that the carrier is preparing for a recession. Frontier Airlines (ULCC) pulled guidance the next day, noting that it expects a loss for the current quarter. Both airlines noted falls in domestic demand and uncertainty over tariffs in their updates. Air Canada (OTCQX:ACDVF) noted sharp declines in US-bound travel from Canada as well, and its stock has been rocked this year. United took a somewhat unusual step this week and gave split guidance based on whether there's a 2025 recession or not. United also noted that US-based international demand was strong. Both scenarios showed the airline making a profit, which I'd personally be skeptical of. However, the market seemed to like it, and airline stocks broadly rallied on the news. And as of my writing this, the bounce is gone, indicating more market fears over airline demand.

Several analysts have recently noted declines in airport traffic. TSA data is noisy and depends on the timing of holidays and big events, but a recent economics note from private equity giant Apollo notes a sharp decline in airport arrivals at the top 10 US airports. The report doesn't explicitly separate international vs. domestic arrivals, but the presumption is that international arrivals are down significantly to the US over customs and immigration fears that have been widely circulated on social media. I think these fears are dramatically overblown in reality, but the data clearly shows bookings to the US falling, particularly from Europe, Canada, and Mexico.

Airlines Do Seem To Be Correctly Reflecting Recession Fears

There's an interesting contrast between airlines noting strength in outbound travel from the US and weakness in domestic travel and arrivals. But since flight purchases are such a discretionary purchase, one would expect airlines to be one of the hardest hit industries in a recession. History is littered with defunct airlines that went bust during or shortly after various recessions. Great airlines of the past like Midwest Airlines and their free chocolate chip cookies are no longer. Now we're left with aluminum tubes shuttling heavily tattooed masses of people to Miami, Las Vegas, and Los Angeles. A recent study found that 60% of Coachella general admission tickets were bought not even on credit, but on buy-now-pay-later plans, which charge customers credit cards over time. Last month, everyone was talking about the buy-now-pay-later financial apparatus created to finance DoorDash (DASH) burrito purchases.

The kinds of financial gymnastics that consumers are doing are not sustainable, and one wonders if student loans recently kicking back in had more of an effect on the airlines than DOGE or tariffs. Even more unsustainable were the post-COVID personal savings rates that have hovered near the pre-2008 crash lows. They're ticking up now, and consumers are finally seeming to reset their expectations closer to their incomes. But to match the levels of the 2010s or 1990s, consumer spending would need to continue to fall considerably. These make me somewhat doubt the idea that United Airlines would still make $2.6 billion in a recession when they've gone bankrupt once and nearly went bankrupt twice in the past 25 years.

And on the topic of recession, the betting odds have recently fluctuated, but Polymarket currently has a 54% chance of a 2025 recession. And the S&P 500 (SPY) at large is still more expensive on a P/E basis than it was before COVID. This makes me question the logic in buying the dip off of very high starting prices. P/E valuations more than doubled from their 2008 lows to their recent highs, and while buying the dip works great in cheap markets, it's a dangerous strategy in expensive ones.

Bottom Line

If stocks overall are so expensive, why are airline stocks so staggeringly cheap? Educated guess here – it's a value trap. Airline stocks are down significantly and appear cheap at first glance, but I expect profits to turn to losses in the recessionary scenario that currently appears most likely. Airline investments are always tempting for the quick profits they can bring– even the plot of the classic 1987 film Wall Street revolves partly around an airline acquisition. Here, however, I think the deck is stacked against hopeful investors buying the dip on airline shares. What do you think? Share your thoughts below in the comments!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ASML Q1 Results: Selloff Is Overdone, It's Time To Buy
data.description:
Article Thesis

ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) reported its first quarter earnings results on Wednesday. Shares sold off, despite the fact that the company beat profit estimates easily. Since ASML Holding had already gotten a lot cheaper before the post-earnings slump on Wednesday, this AI beneficiary is now quite attractively priced, I believe.

Past Coverage

I have written about ASML Holding N.V. in the past here on Seeking Alpha, most recently in January. In that article, I gave ASML Holding a "Buy" rating, with shares declining a little since then, but they performed somewhat better than the broad market over the same time frame. With ASML Holding reporting its Q1 earnings on Wednesday, and with the macro picture and the valuation changing since my last article, it is time to take another look at ASML Holding today.

What Happened?

ASML Holding N.V. reported its Q1 earnings on Wednesday, prior to the market's opening. The headline numbers can be seen in the following screencap from Seeking Alpha:

We see that the company missed the revenue consensus estimate, but only very slightly, by around half a percentage point. For a company delivering a growth rate of almost 50%, I do not think that this is a very meaningful miss at all. On the bottom line, ASML Holding performed better than expected, with earnings per share coming in around 3% higher than what Wall Street analysts were forecasting.

At the time of writing, ASML Holding trades down a little more than 5%, despite the earnings beat. This is despite them reaffirming their previous full-year guidance. Thus, there was no indication of a weak outlook. To me, the market's reaction to ASML's results thus seems overblown, as neither the company's Q1 results nor its full-year outlook were bad at all.

ASML Holding: Strong Performance Despite Macro Headwinds

ASML Holding's results can fluctuate to some degree due to the timing of the deliveries of its expensive lithography machines, but the growth rate during the first quarter was so strong that it can't be explained by timing alone.

Instead, it is pretty clear that ASML Holding continues to benefit from the broad semiconductor industry growth rate, especially from the very strong growth in the AI chips space. Companies like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) sell more and more chips to hyperscalers like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG, GOOGL), but also to startups, government entities, universities, and so on. This means that Nvidia's partners, such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), need to produce more and more chips, which is why they continue to invest in more and more production capacity.

As the clear global leader in the lithography space, ASML Holding benefits from growing market demand for its lithography machines, especially in the extreme ultraviolet light space ("EUV"), where the company is dominant. Chip producers that want to expand their production to benefit from the market's strong essentially have to buy the necessary equipment from ASML Holding, which not only results in strong demand for ASML's machines but also gives ASML a lot of pricing power. The result of ASML Holding's strong pricing power is a quite attractive gross margin that keeps getting better -- it came in at 51.7% in Q4 of 2024 but strengthened to an even better 54.0% in the first quarter of the current year. While gross margins can't expand endlessly, I wouldn't be surprised if ASML Holding saw its gross margin continue to trend up for some time, which should be a nice profit tailwind, all else equal.

Looking at a geographic breakdown of where ASML Holding sold its machines, we see the following:

While Taiwan is a key market for chip production and thus as well for chip production equipment, it is not the biggest market for ASML right now -- ASML sells even more machines to South Korea. South Korea has big chip players like Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) and has the advantage of being less at risk geopolitically. China is a major market for ASML as well, although it remains to be seen how this changes over time due to the ongoing trade struggles. But based on the fact that the company maintained its full-year revenue guidance, it looks like ASML Holding is not expecting a big decline in the China business, despite current trade tensions -- or maybe they see other markets get even stronger, which would be fine as well, of course. The U.S. is a major market for ASML as well, due to chip manufacturing capacity investments by companies such as TSMC, Intel Corporation (INTC), and so on.

For the current quarter, ASML's Q2, the company expects net sales of around €7.5 billion, which would be down marginally, but which would be easily above the previous year's Q2 revenue result. For the year, ASML Holding forecasts revenues of around €33 billion -- from a U.S.-based investor's perspective, that number has gotten better, as the U.S. Dollar has weakened considerably versus the Euro in recent weeks. While €33 billion was around $35 billion not too long ago, €33 billion is equal to a little more than $37 billion today. The same logic applies to ASML Holding's dividend, which is declared in Euro -- if the U.S. Dollar weakens against the Euro, as it did in recent weeks, the dividend grows in U.S. Dollars. The payout is €1.84 right now, which equates to around $2.08 -- a couple of weeks ago, the same dividend in Euro would have been equal to less than $2.00.

ASML Holding's Valuation Has Gotten Attractive

Not too long ago, ASML Holding traded at as much as $1,100 per share. Today, shares are changing hands for just $647, representing a big decline versus the 52-week high. While that is unfortunate for those who bought at or close to the highs, it means that those deploying money right now get a much better valuation. ASML Holding is forecasted to earn $26.90 this year, but based on the fact that the company beat the Q1 estimate easily and that it has now beaten the earnings per share consensus for an impressive 15 quarters in a row, I believe that the analyst consensus estimate could be too low. Based on the constant underestimation by Wall Street in the past, there is, I believe, a good chance that Wall Street is, again, too conservative with its 2025 estimate.

If ASML Holding earns $27 this year, the earnings multiple is 24.0. If AMSL Holding earns $28 this year, the earnings multiple is 23.1. Based on the fact that the company outperformed the estimate for 2024 by a combined $1.30+ (adding up the quarterly beats from last year), a $28 per share result for this year seems possible, although it is, of course, not guaranteed. Even if ASML Holding were to underperform estimates and earn just $26.50, the earnings multiple would only be 24.4 -- not very high for a fast-growing industry leader with excellent fundamentals that benefits a lot from the ongoing AI trend.

ASML's management has been buying back shares regularly in the past, and they will likely continue to do so -- at the current valuation, those buybacks are even better than the company's past buybacks at higher earnings multiples.

Takeaway

ASML Holding has some China exposure, but that doesn't seem to be a major problem despite ongoing trade tensions. Management's guidance for this year has been reiterated, Q1 was strong and better than what was forecasted, and ASML's valuation has come down a lot over the last couple of months -- at around 23x to 25x net earnings, ASML looks like a good investment for long-term oriented investors seeking exposure to this industry leader, I believe.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bank Of Canada Pause Shouldn't Last Long
data.description:
By James Knightley & Francesco Pesole

A great focus on uncertainty by the BoC

The Bank of Canada has left its policy interest rate unchanged at 2.75% despite CPI coming in softer yesterday and anxiety over the economic impact from tariffs. The market had been pricing around a one-third chance of a 25bp cut while economists saw it closer to being a coin toss with 15 banks, including ourselves, favouring 'no change' and 14 expecting a 25bp cut. Our belief was that the tariff carve-out for USMCA compliant products mitigates to some extent in the near-term while the proximity to next week's election also probably played a role in the BoC choosing to keep some ammunition back.

The accompanying press release states that tariffs have “increased uncertainty, diminished prospects for economic growth, and raised inflation expectations”. This makes it very challenging to come up with forecasts. They have a range of scenarios where on one extreme there is a limited tariff environment that temporarily weakens growth and inflation stays close to 2%. The other extreme of an all-out protracted trade war that leads to a deep recession with inflation rising above 3%.

No guidance, but we expect more cuts

There is no guidance from the BoC given such an uncertain period lies ahead. The BoC merely state that “our focus will be on ensuring that Canadians continue to have confidence in price stability through this period of global upheaval.” The BoC have already cut rates from 5.0% down to 2.75% with the BoC most recently cutting 25bp in January and March.

Further cuts are coming though as risks are skewed towards weaker growth while the removal of the carbon consumption tax will depress inflation over the coming year. This will help to mitigate the effects of higher imposed tariffs on American products and allow 25bp cuts at the next two BoC policy meetings in June and July.

CAD curve can steepen further

Market pricing for future BoC moves will follow very closely trade news in the coming weeks. Anyway, by the June meeting, data will have given some clarity about the tariff impact.

The OIS curve is pricing in roughly 16bp for June, and 50bp by the end of the year. If we are right with our view that the BoC will frontload easing in June and July, we expect to see some steepening in the CAD curve as front-end yields remain compressed while the long-end can remain affected by further underperformance in US Treasuries.

The net impact on CAD should be negative, although probably spread out over the next few months. We are not convinced the idiosyncratic pressure on USD is due to be unwound soon (more on our latest monthly FX report), so we still think USD/CAD will attract buyers around 1.40.

Our new USD/CAD forecasts

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Instacart: Macro Weakness Likely To Bite In Coming Quarters
data.description:
Instacart's (NASDAQ:CART) business is steadily progressing at the moment, despite its share price coming under pressure in recent months. I previously suggested that rising customer acquisition costs were a concern. This is likely being driven by increased competition, which may limit Instacart's ability to improve its margins longer term. On a positive note, Instacart's financials continue to improve as the company moves past post-pandemic headwinds.

While Instacart has a solid business, I believe that convergence between categories will become a more important theme over the next decade, significantly changing competitive dynamics. In the near-term, macro weakness is likely to pressure consumer spending and I believe that this will dampen demand for Instacart's services.

Market Conditions

Fulfillment costs are crucial to the profitability of grocery delivery and are driven by pick-and-pack and delivery. An analysis by McKinsey suggests online delivery costs amount to around 17% of a $100 order, which is high relative to the typical single digit margins for grocers. This is an interesting situation, as the majority of online grocery shoppers now prefer home delivery to click and collect. There is also less willingness to bear these costs in the form of fees and higher pricing as delivery becomes viewed as the norm.

Instacart's delivery fees, tips, and markups add roughly 25% to the cost of an order, which is likely a key reason that ecommerce hasn't achieved greater penetration of the grocery vertical. Instacart is pushing for grocers to offer equal pricing online though and is lowering the bar for free delivery. For example, Instacart recently lowered its minimum basket size for free delivery for Instacart+ members from $35 to $10.

While I generally expect this situation to persist, due to the potential for low-cost delivery to drive sales, automation is the only way for this to be done sustainably. Automation is a positive for the grocery delivery category as a whole, but it threatens to undermine Instacart's competitive positioning. For example, Walmart (WMT) is investing in microfulfillment centers and likely wants to control online ordering and delivery.

The more immediate concern for Instacart is its sensitivity to the business cycle and potential weakness in consumer spending. I believe that delivery is a luxury that will be cut in the event of macro weakness. CPG ad spend is also something that is likely to come under pressure in a recession. While a downturn is still hypothetical at this point, tariffs, combined with the precarious state of the labor and housing markets, create a setup with elevated downside risk. Instacart has never navigated a genuine recession, making it difficult to predict what will happen, but I would not be surprised to see a 0-10% drop in revenue in the event of even a small recession.

Instacart Business Updates

Instacart's core grocery business has a dominant position in the market at the moment, as illustrated by its more than 70% share of baskets worth over $75 and the fact it continues to add more new users than competitors. Instacart is also better than its peers at converting small basket customers to large basket customers, making the company an attractive partner for grocers. Within this part of the business, Instacart is focused on growing users and order frequency, in part through a focus on affordability. This includes new service options like Super Saver and Free Pickup and EBT SNAP acceptance.

Instacart is also trying to integrate its business with grocers, potentially creating a sizeable barrier to entry. This includes both things like loyalty program integrations and Instacart's enterprise solutions. The value proposition for grocers is essentially that Instacart's solutions can help accelerate growth. Instacart now has 1,800 retail banners on its marketplace and powers 600 enterprise storefronts. The company also launched 30 net new retailer sites in 2024, more than double the prior year.

There remains a large opportunity internationally, which Instacart now appears to be pursuing more aggressively. For example, Instacart has started to deploy Caper Carts with Coles in Australia. Caper Carts are shopping carts that feature a screen, barcode scanner and electronic payment capabilities.

Advertising is a focus area for Instacart, although growth of this part of the business is only modest. Instacart recently introduced AI-enabled automation tools to help advertisers manage campaigns and optimize ad performance. These tools are currently in pilot with select brands. While improved ad performance and lower barriers to adoption are positives, increased use of AI and automation plays to the strengths of organizations with greater scale. As a result, it is not surprising to see Instacart's advertising growth trailing other retail media networks.

There are now over 7,000 active brands on Instacart's platform, with spend exceeding a $1 billion annual run rate in Q4. Emerging brands are an area of particular strength at the moment. Instacart also has more than 220 Carrot Ads partners, a program where Instacart powers ads on partner properties. These partners contribute inventory and help improve performance.

Financial Analysis

Instacart's GTV increased 10% YoY in Q4, with orders up 11% and average order value declining 1%. The decline in average order value was attributed to restaurant orders. Lower minimum order sizes could also be a headwind to average order value going forward. Instacart believes that its affordability initiatives are a growth driver, though.

Advertising and other revenue increased 10% YoY in Q4. While this growth is solid, it isn't overly impressive given the nascent state of Instacart's advertising business and the strong growth being generated by others in the retail media space at the moment. Instacart's weakness relative to other retail media platforms could just be due to the fact that the company is over indexed to food and beverage advertising, though. Advertising revenue appears to be growing in line with the rest of the business, which may limit Instacart's ability to drive margins substantially higher. The company's long-term target is for ad revenue to total 4-5% of GTV, implying roughly a 50% increase from current levels relative to GTV.

Instacart has given little information about the contribution of restaurants to its business. Restaurants are likely to boost growth while weighing on margins, though. Given Instacart's lack of scale, I question whether its restaurants business is actually profitable at the moment. The company has stated that grocery and restaurants reinforce each other, though, meaning restaurants are likely still viewed as a positive overall.

Instacart expects $9-$9.15 billion GTV in Q1, an increase of 8-10% YoY. While this guidance is somewhat soft, there will be roughly a 1% headwind due to 2024 being a leap year. Given current macro uncertainty, Q2 guidance will be critical. While it may still be too early for buyer behavior to be impacted, I believe that there is downside risk to Instacart's growth.

Instacart generated $252 million in adjusted EBITDA and $153 million of operating cash flow in Q4. Cash flows were depressed by working capital fluctuations in the quarter. Instacart's cash flows are generally improving, though, and the company's operating margins have rebounded in recent quarters.

Instacart expects $220-$230 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q1 at roughly a 25% margin. While I expect Instacart's margins to continue improving with scale, particularly if Instacart has greater success in areas like advertising and enterprise solutions, increased competition could be a headwind in time. Smaller average order sizes could also weigh on margins.

Conclusion

Investor sentiment toward many gig economy companies has improved over the past few years as these companies have transitioned to profitability, or at least begun to generate positive free cash flow. I question whether optimism towards some of these companies is overdone in the short term, though.

Instacart's revenue multiple is modest, and the company ended 2024 with liquidity of $1.5 billion. Instacart is also expected to have a mid-teens earnings multiple within the next few years, despite expectations of long-term double-digit growth.

Grocery delivery is an underpenetrated market that currently has reasonable economics and offers opportunities in a number of adjacent markets. As these markets converge, competition is likely to increase, though, and the basis of competition could shift. In particular, I believe there is a risk that the automation of fulfillment will increase the bargaining power of grocers and that success will then be determined by the company with the largest and most efficient delivery network.

As a result, I find it hard to get excited about Instacart's stock at current prices. Consideration also needs to be given to macro weakness and how this would impact Instacart's business. I believe grocery delivery is a luxury that would be hard hit in a recession, exposing investors to significant downside risk.

data.title: RWJ: Value-Heavy But With Low Quality, Worth Avoiding
data.description:
Today's note is supposed to offer a fresh look at the Invesco S&P SmallCap 600 Revenue ETF (NYSEARCA:RWJ), a passively managed vehicle offering exposure to a reweighted version of the S&P 600, with companies with the largest revenues occupying the top spots and vice versa. This is a rather intriguing proposition, but does RWJ live up to expectations?

I have been covering RWJ since 2021, with three notes presented to date and the most recent one published in May 2024. It is true that I have always struggled to find a compelling reason why RWJ should qualify for a Buy rating, so all these articles concluded that it was a Hold.

The gist is that there were a few concerns, chief among them being its critically small exposure to the quality factor that the revenue-centered weighting schema failed to bolster. And today, there will be no upgrade either.

There are two reasons for that.

  • First, RWJ's recent performance amid the tariff saga, despite having a sizable exposure to the value factor, has been merely awful. Sure, it has somewhat stabilized together with the broad market recently, yet its YTD return remains horrible.
  • Second, quality is still the top concern. And in the note, I would like to illustrate that its revenue-centered weighting has not alleviated but deepened some of the S&P 600's quality issues.

So, in sum, I believe that at this juncture, investors should favor high quality (with no compromises) and value, better combined with the size factor (large caps favored over their smaller counterparts), and, alas, RWJ does not fit in that cohort. A Hold rating is thus maintained, with the top reason why I abstained from a bearish thesis being persistent macro uncertainty.

What is The Basis For RWJ's Strategy?

In short, RWJ's strategy is about the S&P 600 but with a twist. As we know from the ETF's website, the basis for its strategy is the S&P SmallCap 600® Revenue-Weighted Index, which

... is constructed using a rules-based approach that re-weights securities of the S&P SmallCap 600® Index according to the revenue earned by the companies, with a maximum 5% per company weighting. The Fund and Index are rebalanced quarterly.

RWJ Performance

What could be the rationale for applying a revenue-centered weighting schema to the S&P 600? In my view, it can do a few things:

  • Build a portfolio that is "more connected to the economic reality", as I called it in the previous notes.
  • Improve valuation characteristics.
  • Potentially even push the weighted-average beta coefficient of the basket lower.
  • And then improve risk dispersion overall, thus allowing the portfolio to become more resilient and less sensitive to stress.

'Less sensitive to stress' means that its drawdowns should not be that steep compared to the S&P 600 ETF and, ideally, to the market. But this has not been the case this year. The recent rebound aside, it has been a mere ordeal, as RWJ has underperformed both the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) and the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), and substantially.

And since my previous skeptical article, RWJ has also significantly underperformed the S&P 500 index.

Sure, its longer-term returns look brighter than the most recent (if we are looking at the full calendar months only, that obviously does not include April yet).

Nevertheless, even here there are a few concerning issues.

  • Since the fund's reorganization in 2019 (the period covered by the table above is June 2019-March 2025), it has delivered a rather solid annualized return, almost beating IVV. However, as I have already explained in the previous article, it was the effect of its meteoric rise in 2021 (52.84% total return vs. IVV's 28.76% and IJR's 26.6%). And over the January 2022-March 2025 period, its annualized return was 0.88%, while IVV delivered 6.65% and IJR's return was -1.27%.
  • This leads us to an important conclusion: RWJ is highly volatile. Its standard deviation of 29.21% illustrates that nicely.
  • Additionally, its maximum drawdown of 37.95% is also telling.

RWJ Portfolio: Revenue Weighting Boosts Value, Quality Remains A Vulnerability

As of April 14, there were 601 equities in RWJ's basket, with consumer discretionary being the key sector with 24.3% weight.

And as we can see above, since IJR's and RWJ's weighting methods are entirely different, despite them having almost the same holdings (there are minor differences when it comes to cash and derivatives, etc.), they have notably different sector proportions.

For example, RWJ is way lighter in financials, IJR's top sector, while substantially heavier in consumer discretionary. But this is just the tip of the iceberg, as things become more interesting when we delve deeper into the factor intricacies.

Let us start with the size and value factors.

Metric RWJ (holdings as of April 14, 2025)* RWJ (holdings as of May 1, 2024) IJR (holdings as of April 14, 2025)
Market Cap $2.45 billion $2.53 billion $3.09 billion
Adjusted EY 6.92% 6.20% 5.69%
P/S 1.05 1.07 3.73
Quant Valuation B- or higher 57.78% 53.80% 34.72%
Quant Valuation D+ or lower 18.38% 18.70% 39.82%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs. *Financial data as of April 15

RWJ has a substantial edge over its peer, as all the metrics presented illustrate. Sure, revenue weighting did a solid job here.

However, as we have already discussed, its stronger weighted-average adjusted earnings yield and other advantages have done little to shore up its performance this year.

Moving to the next factor, this is where RWJ has something of a perennial issue, as, surprisingly, its unconventional weighting method did not contribute to quality characteristics but, contrarily, detracted from them.

Metric RWJ (holdings as of April 14, 2025) RWJ (holdings as of May 1, 2024) IJR (holdings as of April 14, 2025)
ROA 4.13% 3.01% 4.13%
Adjusted ROE 10.37% 11.90% 12.20%
Quant Profitability B- or higher 35.47% 38% 51.32%
Quant Profitability D+ or lower 22.80% 19.20% 12.34%
FCF negative companies 19.32% 19.43% 14.30%
Loss-making companies 23.32% 21.80% 13.84%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

For example, we see that even adjusted for negative and triple-digit figures, its weighted-average Return on Equity is just at 10.37%, below that of IJR. As a reminder, I prefer to see that figure close to 20%, and IVV mostly delivers on that.

Next, the share of companies with serious red flags on profitability (a D+ Quant Profitability grade or weaker) has risen to 22.8%.

Besides, we see that RWJ is much heavier in loss-making and FCF-negative companies than IJR. Just to give a bit more color on this, an example worth mentioning is The Chemours Company (CC), which has negative trailing twelve-months net operating and free cash flows. CC has TTM revenue of $5.78 billion, which, as per my calculations, is 4.9x larger than the median revenue of the S&P 600's constituents. However, as the company is trading at a notably depressed valuation with a diminutive Price/Sales ratio of 0.3x, it has only 15 bps weight in IJR. At the same time, as revenue is what matters for RWJ, its weight is 2.6x larger at 39 bps.

So RWJ's weighting methodology does not immunize it from low quality. On the contrary, at times it amplifies issues.

The growth factor is also worth touching upon briefly.

Metric RWJ (holdings as of April 14, 2025) RWJ (holdings as of May 1, 2024) IJR (holdings as of April 14, 2025)
EPS Fwd 6.50% 4.10% 11.70%
Revenue Fwd 2.86% 3.30% 7.28%
Holdings With Negative Revenue Fwd 32.20% 33.30% 17.07%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Since my previous analysis, the WA forward EPS growth rate has improved while the forward revenue growth rate has declined. Nevertheless, RWJ's basket is still light in growthier companies, and IJR is far ahead of it thanks to weighting (as, for instance, firms with revenues forecast to decline going forward account for a much smaller part of its portfolio). And this makes me conclude that it was RWJ's poor quality that has mostly driven its losses this year, not the growth premia being trimmed.

Final Thoughts

So, is RWJ a good choice in the current market environment? A seemingly pertinent argument is that it has decent valuation characteristics, and value is the right place to be. However, its recent performance has been hideous, despite it having rather comfortable multiples. And I believe its soft quality is to blame. So, my conclusion today is that it is worth avoiding this smart-beta vehicle.

data.title: Sandvik AB (publ) 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Sandvik AB (publ) in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Estee Lauder Faces Challenges, And It Can Get Worse
data.description:
The Estée Lauder Companies Inc. (NYSE:EL) is a giant in the beauty industry and owns renowned brands, but it is still going through a turbulent period. In part due to its above-average historical profitability rating within its sector (B from Seeking Alpha quant, though it was higher when I began to follow it), I've been tracking the stock since early 2024. It came close to making it through the door into my portfolio, but I'm glad I kept it on the outer aisle of the store, so to speak. Because some stocks can attract buying interest amid a series of negative corporate events. But not this one. This one "stinks" as an investment. At least it has since peaking at $370 to close 2022.

But then, as if it were aiming to do its impersonation of a past-its-prime mall store that sells video games, or a movie theater chain in disrepair, EL did what my late father used to say when his sports team blew a big lead (the NY Knicks, usually): "EL foldo." Yes, EL stock has folded in half multiple times since then. Down 85% in just 15 months. What happened, and will it be a "big dipper" to buy at some point?

You can't put lipstick on this pig of a stock

I don't usually start with technical charts, but for a once-prominent stock and industry leader, there have been no breaks, no bounces that last long enough to enjoy. And, no imminent signs of bottoming.

The longer-term chart (monthly prices) has a usual feature which sums up EL in my second language, technicals: I don't see a PPO (lower indicator) at -30 very often. That's not undervalued, it is underground.

But stock price charts are not everything, even if they are the main thing to me. The fundamental chart below shows a few of the reasons the stock has given up the vast majority of its market capitalization in such a short time. Some reflections of this are revenue declining, cash generation decreasing, and debt rising.

That will cause a stock to give back 13 years or market cap gains in about 13 months.

EL Faces Structural Challenges

There are several reasons for this. In the last quarter, the company's revenue fell by 11% in the Asia/Pacific region, while in the other regions, the decrease was closer to the low single digit. In the breakdown by category, skincare, which is the core of the Estée Lauder Companies, fell by 12%, while make-up was stable.

And this doesn't seem to be coming to an end. Recently, expectations of a continuation of this pressure on sales in Asia were mentioned, and as a consequence, the company plans to make more job cuts.

While these challenges may seem short-term, and in part they are, the company also faces structural challenges. For example, the company is still dependent on the Asian market, and as long as there is uncertainty in this market, as well as global macroeconomic headwinds, it is possible that the fundamentals will continue to slip.

Another crucial factor is competition, not only from other traditional giant players, but also from new, less conventional methods, such as influencer brands, which are gaining ground and consequently taking market share away from more traditional brands.

As a response to these problems, the company has initiated the "Beauty Reimagined" strategic plan, which aims to maintain its focus on the consumer, accelerate innovation, increase investments, and of course, seek the consequence of increasing growth and margins. Although it's good to see that management is seeing the challenges and trying to remain active, the results of these efforts are still quite uncertain, especially given the dynamism of the current market.

EL Valuation Isn’t That Attractive

The highest EPS EL achieved was in fiscal year 2021, reaching a figure of $7.79. This figure would give us a price-to-earnings of 7x, a very attractive level for this industry. But still, that would be the company at full potential, far from what we're seeing now.

As the market constantly revises the outlook according to the current scenario, the expectation now is for a forward price-to-earnings of 22x in view of the fiscal year 2026 EPS, and even in 2028, this multiple only drops to something close to 14.5x.

The valuation may seem very attractive, but it's really only for those who have enough confidence in the company's turnaround. That's not the case for me.

Now, EL is a behemoth in this industry and has the capacity to carry out these optimizations. But this market is changing a lot, and being exposed to these risks and high uncertainty is not interesting to me in the long term.

Factor grades manage to summarize part of this story, indicating only poor valuation, but horrible growth.

Why and how I could end up owning EL anyway

If you are scratching your head about that sub-header, based on every single disparaging word you just read, allow me to explain. I actually have 2 scenarios in which EL could land in my personal portfolio.

1. "Dog Collar:" A quick re-introduction to this approach. Option collars are not new, but my take on it has a bit of a wrinkle in it (pun very much intended!). This involves taking a stock that is badly sold off (like this one is), buying it, but protecting the position by buying puts and selling calls around it. I'm not doing this right now with EL, but here's an example of what it might look like, based on data as of Tuesday's market close:

This is one of many examples, and the pricing on this one is not great, but I'm not buying it now anyway. It also only goes out to 9/19/25, which is short for a stock whose recovery will not be.

So for demonstration purposes, EL is a $54 stock, and I can spend $5.44 a share (per 100 shares I own) to get a combination of puts struck at $55 and calls struck at $70. Bottom line: I can sell the stock at a net price of around $50 a share during this period, and if it rallies to $70, I could be forced to sell it. About 19% upside and 9% downside risk from current levels. If an investor had done this a year ago, they could have limited their loss to as little as 5-10% (it is all customizable), and skated from the 60% decline in the actual stock price.

2. Long-term contrarian stock basket: this is a work in progress at the investing group I run here, but my mission is to build for myself and show subscribers a group of perhaps 20-30 stocks I think can potentially double within 3 years' time. The market's latest move downward simply accelerated my process of filling in the list. But I'm also not convinced I won't get many of them lower. The positions I will take will be very small, and potentially layered in, bought in multiple tranches for each stock. And with a stock basket of that size, the idea is to expect some to continue to implode, but have a small number of them take off over that period. Fundamental strength and/or deep discounted valuation is a part of the equation. Will EL be part of that? I don't know yet. But is it on the list? Definitely.

EL loco?

Here's why: because in the stock market, I never say never. Which is why I'm surprised how often I hear it in the comments section here. I just don't think that way. The merger, new management, a change of fortune few expected, or some other event can turn stocks on a dime. The nature of today's market, much to my chagrin, is that sometimes, all it takes is some buying interest from a motivated, deep-pocket contrarian to turn the tide. No, I don't expect to ever consider EL a "meme stock." But will it be a flyer to consider? Never say never.
